title: RSS 2.0, Atom 1.0, Namespaces, Text Constructs, and Extensions · Scenario Checkpoint
summary: A release reviewer compares near-miss TypeScript decisions for rss 2.0, atom 1.0, namespaces, text constructs, and extensions and accepts only runtime and compiler evidence, explicit non-claims, and named browser, Node, network, XML, PostgreSQL, handle, load, accessibility, deployment, and production transfer gates. Planned artifact: Record independent decisions and explanations for format-aware syndication model. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, cau…
objectives: Model RSS channel and item requirements, GUID semantics, dates, links, authors, categories, enclosures, source, and optional variance.
Model Atom feed and entry IDs, authors, links, text and content constructs, categories, dates, xml:base, xml:lang, and source.
Interpret expanded namespace names independently from chosen prefixes and preserve unsupported extension provenance.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Feed aggregation and TypeScript Feed Ts Rss Semantics · changed case · Quiz Feed Ts Rss Semantics
steps.instruction:
quiz case — A quiz TypeScript team handles case 8927 during a hostile XML and unknown-value admission drill. Build deterministic TypeScript evidence to prove RSS and Atom semantics, namespaces, content variance, and extension restraint; publisher fetch and retention budgets must remain explicit; then change one RSS, Atom, namespace, entity, date, identity, or runtime-validation case and reconcile provenance. Browser TypeScript uses pure bounded models and original RSS, Atom, HTTP, PostgreSQL, and reader fixtures only. Real DNS, Fetch, streams, fast-xml-parser, Express, PostgreSQL, processes, signals, handle and load claims, containers, accessibility review, backups, and production behavior require explicit controlled transfer gates. Competency probe: Model RSS channel and item requirements, GUID semantics, dates, links, authors, categories, enclosures, source, and optional variance.
